unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Food is one of Nature's great blessings. In this unit you will learn how to talk about food and health in English. You will read about where food comes from, how to keep it clean and safe, and why fresh food helps your body and your brain. You will learn to give clear instructions, ask good questions, and compare healthy and unhealthy choices. Along the way you will meet many new words — from sandwich and rice to pesticide and famine — and you will use grammar that helps you explain rules, reasons, and comparisons. By the end, you will be ready to read this unit's story, “The Bitter Lunch”, about a boy named Sami who learns to check his food with care.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and tick your Word Wall.
Work through the six short lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time.
Read the Story file, “The Bitter Lunch”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Children discussing balanced food, health and active choices

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Food made of two pieces of bread with meat, cheese, or vegetables inside.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.
practiceSentences: I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.
Theo shared his beef sandwich with his friend.
What filling makes the tastiest sandwich, cheese or egg?
"Cut the sandwich in half, please," said Maya.
I made a giant sandwich with salad and olives.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - n - d - w - i - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sandwich', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A young sheep, or the meat from it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.
practiceSentences: We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.
The butcher sold fresh lamb for the harvest feast.
Does Grandmother's lamb soup smell wonderful today?
"This lamb is tender and full of flavour," said Uncle.
I tasted grilled lamb at my cousin's wedding.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - m - b
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'lamb', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Small white or brown grains that people cook and eat.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She ate rice with her chicken.
practiceSentences: She ate rice with her chicken.
Steamed rice with vegetables is our Friday family meal.
How much rice should we cook for six people?
"Wash the rice before you cook it," said Grandmother.
I helped scoop rice onto every guest's plate.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - i - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rice', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A part of a plant, like pepper, that adds flavour to food.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We added some spice to make the soup tastier.
practiceSentences: We added some spice to make the soup tastier.
Cinnamon is a sweet spice used in many cakes.
What spice gives the curry its golden colour?
"Add one spoon of spice, not three," laughed Mother.
I smelled every spice jar in Grandmother's kitchen.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - p - i - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'spice', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where bread and cakes are made and sold.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We bought fresh bread from the bakery.
practiceSentences: We bought fresh bread from the bakery.
The bakery fills our street with the smell of bread.
Does the bakery sell date cookies on Fridays?
"Meet me outside the bakery after school," said Samira.
I bought warm rolls from the bakery for breakfast.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - a - k - e - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'bakery', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: New and clean; not old or spoiled.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I love to eat fresh fruit from the market.
practiceSentences: I love to eat fresh fruit from the market.
The market sells fresh vegetables straight from the farm.
Why does fresh bread smell so much better than old bread?
"Drink this fresh orange juice," said Aunt Maryam.
I picked fresh mint from our garden for the tea.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - e - s - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fresh', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Hard to bite, so you need to chew it a lot.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The dried meat was very chewy.
practiceSentences: The dried meat was very chewy.
The toffee was so chewy that Sami's jaw ached.
Do you like chewy dates or soft ones better?
"This bread crust is lovely and chewy," said Father.
I found the dried mango chewy but very tasty.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - h - e - w - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'chewy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very dangerous; able to cause death.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Some wild mushrooms are deadly, so we never eat them.
practiceSentences: Some wild mushrooms are deadly, so we never eat them.
The teacher explained that some berries are deadly to eat.
Which snake has the most deadly bite in Africa?
"That plant is deadly, so never touch it," warned the guide.
I learned that deadly germs hide on unwashed hands.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - a - d - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'deadly', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To collect things or bring people together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We gather ripe dates from the palm trees.
practiceSentences: We gather ripe dates from the palm trees.
The farmers gather wheat before the rainy season begins.
Where do the families gather for the summer festival?
"Help me gather the fallen apples," said Grandfather.
I love it when we gather around Grandmother for stories.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - a - t - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'gather', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The part of your body inside your head that helps you think and learn.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Good food helps my brain think clearly.
practiceSentences: Good food helps my brain think clearly.
Your brain sends messages to every part of your body.
How does sleep help your brain remember new things?
"Puzzles give your brain good exercise," said Mrs Ali.
I fed my brain with a good book before bed.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - r - a - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'brain', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The part of your body where food goes after you eat it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My stomach hurt after I ate too much candy.
practiceSentences: My stomach hurt after I ate too much candy.
Maya's stomach rumbled loudly just before the lunch bell rang.
Why does your stomach growl when you are hungry?
"Eat slowly so your stomach can rest," said the doctor.
My stomach felt full after the big birthday breakfast.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - o - m - a - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'stomach', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A serious sickness where some of the body's cells grow the wrong way.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Doctors and nurses work hard to help people with cancer.
practiceSentences: Doctors and nurses work hard to help people with cancer.
Doctors use special medicine to fight cancer in the body.
How do hospitals help people who have cancer?
"Eating vegetables may help protect against cancer," said the nurse.
We made kind cards for children with cancer at the hospital.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - n - c - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'cancer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A drink that changes how adults act; it is not safe for children.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Adults are told that too much alcohol is unhealthy.
practiceSentences: Adults are told that too much alcohol is unhealthy.
Muslims do not drink alcohol because it harms the body.
Why is alcohol so dangerous for a person's health?
"Alcohol is never served in our home," said Father.
I learned that alcohol can damage the liver and brain.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - l - c - o - h - o - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'alcohol', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Large farm animals such as cows and bulls, kept for milk and meat.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The farmer led his cattle to the field.
practiceSentences: The farmer led his cattle to the field.
The cattle drank from the stream near the meadow.
How many cattle does the farmer bring home each evening?
"Move the cattle to the shady field," said the farmer.
I heard the cattle mooing softly at sunrise.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - t - t - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'cattle', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Hard work, or the people who do it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The labor of farming starts early in the morning.
practiceSentences: The labor of farming starts early in the morning.
Building the barn took months of hard labor.
Why does farm labor begin before the sun rises?
"Honest labor brings its own reward," said Grandfather.
I respect the labor that brings food to our table.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - b - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'labor', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A chemical used to kill insects that harm plants.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Farmers use pesticide to protect their crops.
practiceSentences: Farmers use pesticide to protect their crops.
The farmer sprayed pesticide carefully to save his tomato plants.
Can too much pesticide harm the bees and birds?
"Wash the fruit in case of pesticide," said Mother.
I read that some farms grow food without any pesticide.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - s - t - i - c - i - d - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'pesticide', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Substances made from different materials; some can be harmful.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Too many chemicals on food can be dangerous.
practiceSentences: Too many chemicals on food can be dangerous.
Strong chemicals must be kept away from little children.
Which chemicals do farmers spray on their fields?
"Wear gloves when using cleaning chemicals," said Father.
I learned that some chemicals can pollute the river.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - h - e - m - i - c - a - l - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'chemicals', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A time when many people do not have enough food to eat.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: During the famine, families shared what little they had.
practiceSentences: During the famine, families shared what little they had.
The village stored grain to prepare for a famine.
How can countries help each other during a famine?
"We send food to lands facing famine," said the charity worker.
My grandfather told stories about surviving a terrible famine.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - m - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'famine', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Money that you owe to someone else.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The farmer worked hard to pay back his debt.
practiceSentences: The farmer worked hard to pay back his debt.
The shopkeeper finally paid off his debt this year.
Is it wise to borrow money and carry a debt?
"Always repay a debt quickly," advised my grandfather.
I paid my small debt to Theo with two marbles.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - b - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'debt', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small mark ( , ) used to show a pause or to separate items in a list.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I like apples, bananas, and oranges.
practiceSentences: I like apples, bananas, and oranges.
A comma tells the reader to pause for a moment.
Where should the comma go in this long sentence?
"Put a comma between the items in your list," said Mrs Ali.
I forgot the comma, so my sentence looked confusing.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - m - a
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'comma', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe many foods, and talk about where food comes from.
Explain why fresh, clean food is good for your body and your brain.
Give clear instructions using imperatives (“Wash the fruit.”).
Use must and might to talk about rules and possibilities.
Ask Yes/No questions and Wh- questions about food and health.
Explain reasons using the infinitive of purpose (“We eat to grow.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Compare foods and choices (“Fresh food is better than old food.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write instructions, a health poster, a short report, and a short story.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: From Farm to Plate
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Every meal begins far away from your plate. Farmers work hard to grow rice in wet fields and to keep cattle in dry ones. Their labor starts early in the morning and ends when the sun goes down. When the food is ready, it travels to the market, the bakery, and finally to your home. Fresh food is full of goodness. It helps your body grow and helps your brain think clearly. But food must be kept clean. Sometimes farmers use pesticide to stop insects from eating the crops. A little can protect the plants, but too many chemicals can be harmful. That is why we must always wash fruit and vegetables before we eat them.

readings.title: A Poster on the Wall
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sami's teacher put this health poster on the classroom wall. Read it:
Eat fresh food to stay strong.  ·  Wash your hands before you eat.  ·  Drink clean water every day.  ·  Fresh fruit is better than sweets.  ·  Never eat food that smells strange.

readings.title: At the Health Center
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Nurse:  “Good morning. How do you feel today?”
Sami:  “My stomach hurts. I ate an old sandwich last night.”
Nurse:  “Was the meat fresh?”
Sami:  “No. It was not fresh, and it smelled strange.”
Nurse:  “You must be more careful. Old food might make you very sick. Always check your food first.”

readings.title: The Market Song
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Rice and lamb and fresh green peas, gather them with care, if you please! Wash them well and cook them slow — healthy food helps children grow!”

readings.title: The Bitter Lunch
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In the hot fields of Sudan, Sami helped his uncle grow rice and care for the cattle. Every morning before school, he fed the cows and carried water from the well. It was hard labor, but his uncle always smiled and said, “We work hard now to eat well later.”
One day at school, Sami's teacher stood at the front of the class. “I have a project for you,” she said. “Each of you will write a story about food, and it must carry a health message. You must use our new words and write with care. You might even share your story at the community lunch.”
Sami raised his hand. “Can it be a true story?” he asked.
“Yes,” the teacher said, “as long as it teaches something helpful.”
That evening, Sami walked to the village bakery. It smelled of warm bread. He was hungry, so he bought a sandwich filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of spice.
“I should tell you,” said the baker, “this meat is not fresh. But it still tastes good.” Sami was too hungry to care.
At home, he shared part of the sandwich with his cousin Sarah. She frowned. “I think this meat is from sick cattle,” she said. “There is a strange smell.”
“You worry too much,” said Sami, and he ate the rest.
Late that night, Sami's stomach hurt badly. He felt heat in his belly and cold in his hands, and he could not sleep. Sarah heard him groaning and woke her father.
At the health center, a kind nurse checked him carefully. “This may be food poisoning,” she said. “Some chemicals are used to clean old meat, and they can be dangerous if they are not washed off.”
Sami groaned. “I only ate a sandwich. It had a lot of spice, but the meat may have been old.”
“You must be more careful,” said the nurse gently. “Old meat and dirty tools can lead to disease. Eaten too often, bad food can even lead to a serious sickness like cancer. Always check your food first.”
The next day, Sami stayed home from school. His uncle brought him warm soup and said, “You are lucky this was not deadly. Always check what you eat.”
By the afternoon, Sami felt better. Then he remembered his story. He sat outside with his notebook and wrote:
“I once ate a bad sandwich. It looked fine, but it was not fresh. It gave me a sore stomach, and I learned to check what I eat.”
He thought for a while, then wrote more: “Some chemicals help to clean food, but too much is harmful. Farmers must use less pesticide, and people must wash their food with care.”
“What else should I write?” he asked Sarah.
“Gather some facts about food and health,” she said. “Then give good advice.”
Sami smiled. “That helps.” He added a final part: “We eat to feed our brain and our body, and good food helps us grow. Too much alcohol or dirty food can make us sick. A famine shows us how precious food really is, and how we must protect it.”
On the day of the community lunch, Sami read his story aloud. A few children laughed at first — until he reached the part about his illness. Then the room grew quiet, and everyone listened.
“Thank you for that, Sami,” said the teacher. “Now, who else would like to share?”
Hannah raised her hand. “Sami also learned where to put a comma in a list of food!” The whole room giggled.
Then the village elder stood up. “Food is a gift we must not waste,” he said. “Let us not waste it, and let us never eat without checking it first.”
As the food was served — lamb, rice, and sweet dates — Sami chose only what looked clean and safe. The meal was simple, but everything was warm and fresh.
Sami leaned over to Sarah. “This is the freshest lamb I have had in weeks,” he whispered.
She grinned. “That is because I helped to cook it.”
Sami took a bite and smiled. This time, his stomach did not hurt at all.

grammar.title: Modal Verbs — must and might
grammar.explanation: Modal verbs are special helping verbs. In this unit we use two of them: must and might. Must shows that something is necessary or very important — a rule. Might shows that something is possible, but not certain. Knowing both helps you explain rules and possibilities clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
must  →  subject + must + base verb:  “You must wash fresh fruit.”
might  →  subject + might + base verb:  “Pesticides might harm plants.”
Negatives: mustn't means “not allowed” (“You mustn't eat old meat.”). might not means “probably won't” (“She might not come today.”).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You must to wash your hands.”  ✗
Fix: After must or might, use the base verb with no “to”: “You must wash your hands.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of must as a strong rule from a teacher (“You must!”), and might as a gentle “maybe” from a friend (“We might…”).
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with must or might: | You ______ finish your lunch before going outside. (a rule) | It ______ rain tomorrow, so bring a jacket! (a possibility) | We ______ wash the vegetables before we cook them. (a rule) | She ______ go to the market later if she has time. (a possibility)

grammar.title: Yes/No Questions
grammar.explanation: A Yes/No question is a question that can be answered with “yes” or “no”. It helps you get clear, direct information — for example, “Is this meat fresh?” → “Yes, it is.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with a helping verb (is, are, do, does, can), then the subject, then the rest:
Is alcohol bad for health?
Does rice grow in water?
Are you hungry?
Can we buy bread at the bakery?
Use do with I, you, we, they. Use does with he, she, it.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Does he eats breakfast?”  ✗
Fix: After does, use the base verb with no “s”: “Does he eat breakfast?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Remember the order: Helping verb + subject + the rest. Say it like a little rhyme.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct helping verb (Do, Does, Are, Is) to start each question: | ______ cats eat fish? | ______ your brother like rice? | ______ they hungry now? | ______ it raining outside? | ______ you want more soup?

grammar.title: The Infinitive of Purpose
grammar.explanation: The infinitive of purpose explains why we do something. When we say, “We eat vitamins to stay healthy,” the words “to stay healthy” tell us the reason. It makes your sentences clear and meaningful.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use to + the base verb to give the reason:
Action + purpose:  “Farmers work to grow crops.”
“We eat good food to help our brain and body.”
“She saves money to buy fresh food.”
You can also say so that with a subject: “We wash food so that we do not get sick.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “We eat vitamins staying healthy.”  ✗   (no “to”, wrong verb form)
Fix: Use to + the base verb: “We eat vitamins to stay healthy.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of to as a little bridge that joins the action to the reason. Cross the bridge with “to” + a verb.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with “to” + the verb in brackets: | He exercises every day ______ (keep) fit. | She saves her money ______ (buy) fresh fruit. | We wash our hands ______ (stay) clean. | They study hard ______ (improve) their English.

grammar.title: Imperatives (Giving Commands)
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are verbs used to give a command, an instruction, or advice. They are perfect for recipes, posters, and safety rules — “Wash the fruit.” “Gather the cattle.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the base verb, and leave out the subject “you”: Gather the cattle!
Add details to be clear: Avoid deadly plants.  ·  Carefully wash the meat.
For a command not to do something, use Don't: Don't eat old food.
Be polite by adding please: Please close the door.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You gather the cattle!”  ✗   (do not include “you”)
Fix: Start with the verb: “Gather the cattle!”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: An imperative is an action word giving a direct order — like a coach shouting one clear word: “Run!” “Stop!”
grammar.practice: Write the imperative using the verb in brackets: | ______ (eat) your vegetables! | ______ (wash) the fruit before you eat it. | ______ (not / touch) that hot pot! | ______ (help) your brother carry the rice.

grammar.title: Comparisons
grammar.explanation: Comparisons show how two things are the same or different. They help you share opinions and make choices — for example, “Fresh food is better than old food.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short adjective (one syllable): add -er.  tall → taller,  fresh → fresher.
Adjective ending in -y: change y to -ier.  happy → happier,  healthy → healthier.
Long adjective (three or more syllables): use more.  delicious → more delicious.
Then add than:  “Rice is cheaper than meat.”
Two special ones to remember: good → better, bad → worse.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She is more tall than her brother.”  ✗
Fix: Short adjectives add -er: “She is taller than her brother.”  ✓   Also remember than: not “This car is faster.” but “This car is faster than that one.”
grammar.memoryTip: Remember the rhyme: “For one small word, just add an -er; for a long one, use more, for sure!”
grammar.practice: Write the correct comparison word: | This mango is ______ (sweet) than that one. | Fresh bread is ______ (good) than old bread. | Running is ______ (fun) than walking. | Summer is ______ (hot) than spring.

grammar.title: Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: Wh- questions start with what, who, where, when, why, or how. They help you ask for details and learn more — “Why is famine dangerous?” “How do chemicals affect food?”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What — things:  “What do you like to eat?”
Who — people:  “Who cooked the lamb?”
Where — places:  “Where does rice grow?”
When — time:  “When is dinner?”
Why — reasons:  “Why is famine dangerous?”
How — the way/process:  “How do chemicals affect food?”
With the verb “to be”, it comes straight after the Wh- word: “Where is the bakery?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Why he is running?”  ✗
Fix: Put the verb in the right order: “Why is he running?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of a Wh- word as a fishing rod: each one helps you “fish” for a different kind of answer.
grammar.practice: Complete each question with the correct Wh- word: | ______ do you want for lunch? | ______ is your favourite fruit? | ______ does rice grow? | ______ do we wash our food?  (Ask for the reason.) | ______ do you feel today?

speaking.title: Speaking 1: My Favourite Meal
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to talk for one minute about a meal you love. Try to use these sentences:
“My favourite meal is ______.”
“It is made with ______ and ______.”
“We eat it to ______.”  (a reason — see Grammar Lesson 3)
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the food clearly? Did you give a reason? Did you speak loudly enough?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise my favourite meal and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Giving Healthy Advice
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Imagine your younger cousin is about to eat. Give three commands (imperatives) to help them stay healthy:
“______ your hands first.”
“______ the fruit before you eat it.”
“Don't ______ food that smells strange.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise giving healthy advice and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Healthy or Not?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person names a food or a habit, for example “fresh mangoes” or “too much candy”.
The other person answers with a comparison: “Fresh mangoes are better than candy because ______.”
Swap turns. Try to use the words fresh, deadly, healthy, and better.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise healthy or not? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: How to Make a Sandwich (Instructions)
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four to six steps that explain how to make a healthy sandwich. Use imperatives (command verbs) and order words like first, next, then, and finally:
First, wash your hands.
Next, take two slices of fresh bread.
Then, ______.
Finally, ______.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about how to make a sandwich (instructions).
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: A Health Poster
writing.promptAndInstructions: Design a poster titled “Why Fresh Food is Important”. Write four short pieces of advice. Use at least one comparison (“Fresh food is better than …”) and one sentence with must.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a health poster.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: A Short Report
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write five to six sentences for a mini-report called “How Pesticides Help and Harm”. Explain one way pesticides help farmers and one way too many chemicals can harm people. End with your own advice using must.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a short report.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 4: Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 5: Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 6: Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

activities.title: Activities 1: True or False (Food & Health Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
Fresh food is better for your body than old food.
You should never wash fruit before eating it.
A pesticide is a chemical that kills insects on plants.
Alcohol is a safe drink for children.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Sort the Words
activities.instructionsAndItems: Make two lists from these words: fresh, deadly, chewy, sandwich, rice, stomach. One list is for naming words (nouns) and one is for describing words (adjectives). Check with your AI tutor.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Fill in the Blanks (must / might)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose must or might for each sentence:
You ______ wash your hands before you eat. (a rule)
It ______ rain later, so the farmers are working fast. (a possibility)
We ______ check that meat is fresh before we cook it. (a rule)
She ______ buy bread from the bakery if it is open. (a possibility)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Ask the Question
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write a Wh- question for each answer:
______________________?  →  “Rice grows in wet fields.”  (Where …)
______________________?  →  “Farmers use pesticide to protect the crops.”  (Why …)
______________________?  →  “A famine happens when there is not enough food.”  (What …)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Food and Health learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: How to Make a Sandwich (Instructions) and a recording of Speaking 1: My Favourite Meal. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'sandwich' mean?
quizzes.options: A part of a plant, like pepper, that adds flavour to food. | A place where bread and cakes are made and sold. | Food made of two pieces of bread with meat, cheese, or vegetables inside. | Small white or brown grains that people cook and eat.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Food made of two pieces of bread with meat, cheese, or vegetables inside.
quizzes.explanation: I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.

quizzes.question: What does 'lamb' mean?
quizzes.options: A part of a plant, like pepper, that adds flavour to food. | A place where bread and cakes are made and sold. | A young sheep, or the meat from it. | New and clean; not old or spoiled.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A young sheep, or the meat from it.
quizzes.explanation: We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.

quizzes.question: What does 'rice' mean?
quizzes.options: A place where bread and cakes are made and sold. | Hard to bite, so you need to chew it a lot. | New and clean; not old or spoiled. | Small white or brown grains that people cook and eat.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Small white or brown grains that people cook and eat.
quizzes.explanation: She ate rice with her chicken.

quizzes.question: What does 'spice' mean?
quizzes.options: A part of a plant, like pepper, that adds flavour to food. | Hard to bite, so you need to chew it a lot. | New and clean; not old or spoiled. | Very dangerous; able to cause death.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A part of a plant, like pepper, that adds flavour to food.
quizzes.explanation: We added some spice to make the soup tastier.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'At the Health Center': I ate an old _____ last night.” Nurse: “Was the meat fresh?” Sami: “No.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: I ate an old sandwich last night.” Nurse: “Was the meat fresh?” Sami: “No.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Market Song': “Rice and _____ and fresh green peas, gather them with care, if you please!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Rice and lamb and fresh green peas, gather them with care, if you please!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Farm to Plate': Farmers work hard to grow _____ in wet fields and to keep cattle in dry ones.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Farmers work hard to grow rice in wet fields and to keep cattle in dry ones.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Bitter Lunch': He was hungry, so he bought a sandwich filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: He was hungry, so he bought a sandwich filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of spice.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Farm to Plate': When the food is ready, it travels to the market, the _____, and finally to your home.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: When the food is ready, it travels to the market, the bakery, and finally to your home.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'A Poster on the Wall': Read it: Eat _____ food to stay strong.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Read it: Eat fresh food to stay strong.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read ‘From Farm to Plate’ with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you play a listening game where I answer questions about food and health?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise talking about my favourite meal for one minute?”
“Can you play the ‘Healthy or Not?’ game with me?”
“Can you correct me gently if I say a food word wrong?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my sandwich instructions and see if the steps are in a clear order?”
“Can you help me make my health poster short and strong?”
“Can you check that I used commas correctly in my lists?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on these five food words?”
“Can you help me say ‘bakery’ and ‘sandwich’ correctly?”
“What spices are used in Somali or East African cooking?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between fresh food and old food?”
“Can you give me one more sentence for each of these words?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Why is fresh food good for my brain and body?”
“Can you explain, in a simple way, why some foods and drinks are not healthy?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Why do farmers sometimes use pesticide, and when can chemicals be harmful?”
“Can you explain what a famine is and how people help each other during one?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check that I used commas correctly in my food list?”
“Where else should I use a comma when I write?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘You must wash’ but not ‘You must to wash’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise comparisons about food?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Food made of two pieces of bread with meat, cheese, or vegetables inside. I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.
A young sheep, or the meat from it. We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.
Small white or brown grains that people cook and eat. She ate rice with her chicken.
A part of a plant, like pepper, that adds flavour to food. We added some spice to make the soup tastier.
A place where bread and cakes are made and sold. We bought fresh bread from the bakery.
New and clean; not old or spoiled. I love to eat fresh fruit from the market.
Hard to bite, so you need to chew it a lot. The dried meat was very chewy.
Very dangerous; able to cause death. Some wild mushrooms are deadly, so we never eat them.
To collect things or bring people together. We gather ripe dates from the palm trees.
The part of your body inside your head that helps you think and learn. Good food helps my brain think clearly.
